channel.title: ThingSource — Curious Origins & Accidental Genius
channel.description: An automated daily blog researching the surprising origin of everyday things.

item.title: The Mystery Word: How 'Quiz' Went From Graffiti to Global Phenomenon
item.description: The word 'quiz' — meaning a test or examination — has one of the most debated and murky etymologies in the English language, with a legendary (if possibly apocryphal) origin story involving a Dublin theater owner, a bet, and a city-wide act of chalk vandalism. Tracing its journey reveals as much about how language evolves as it does about 18th-century urban mischief.

item.title: You're Not Paid in Salt — But the Word 'Salary' Might Be
item.description: Every payday, millions of people unknowingly invoke an ancient Roman practice when they cash their 'salary.' The origin of this word is one of the most debated etymological mysteries in the English language, tangled up with legions, salt routes, and a surprisingly persistent myth.

item.title: Caesar Salad Was Invented in Mexico by an Italian Immigrant — And It Was an Accident
item.description: The Caesar salad, a staple of upscale American restaurants, was not invented by Julius Caesar, a Roman chef, or even an American cook. It was thrown together in a panic in 1924 by a Tijuana restaurant owner who was running out of ingredients during a rush.

item.title: The Accidental Condiment: How a Failed Experiment Became Britain's Most Mysterious Sauce
item.description: Worcestershire sauce is one of the world's most beloved condiments, found in kitchens from Tokyo to Texas — but almost nobody knows it was a complete accident that sat fermenting in a cellar for nearly two years before anyone realized it was edible.

item.title: The Sweet Secret: Unwrapping the Surprising True Origin of the Fortune Cookie
item.description: That delightful fortune cookie you crack open after your meal? Its history is far more complex and culturally interwoven than you might think, starting not in China, but with Japanese immigrants in America.
